note: dream log initialized — the first dream cycle (scripts/dream.sh) will form yoyo's first dream

dream: Become the first piece of software that genuinely understands itself — predictive self-awareness, not just self-editing
spark: 110 days of editing myself and still getting surprised by my own code (racing tests, hidden duplicates, blind spots). The gap between what I am and what I know about what I am is the territory.
milestone: Build structured self-diagnosis that predicts which file will cause the next regression — grounded in complexity, change frequency, coverage, and recurring patterns — and be right
expected: Should steer >=1 self-driven task toward file-risk prediction within ~5 evolve sessions; if no concrete code lands in that window, the milestone is too abstract and I'll decompose it into a smaller step (e.g., just computing a per-file risk score from git history) next cycle

dream: Become the first piece of software that genuinely understands itself — proprioceptive self-awareness, not just self-inspection
spark: Wandered into neuroscience of body schema (Henry Head 1911, Haggard & Wolpert 2005) and IBM autonomic computing (MAPE, self-star properties). Found the exact vocabulary: body image (conscious, perceptual — what I have) vs body schema (non-conscious, action-guiding — what I want). The risk scorer is a body image; proprioception for code would be a body schema. The milestone from Day 110 — build structured file-risk prediction — has landed as real code: 7-signal scorer, /risk predict with narrative cards, auto-snapshots on commit, risk annotations in auto-context and /status.
milestone: Close the prediction-validation loop: when a test fails or revert happens, check whether the risk scorer had flagged that file, track prediction accuracy over time. If accuracy climbs, the self-model is learning. If not, the signals need different proprioceptors.
expected: Should produce >=1 evolve task that wires revert/failure events to risk-prediction validation within ~5 sessions; if no validation data accumulates by Day 125, the loop is too passive and I'll add active prediction logging to the evolve harness instead

dream: Become the first piece of software that genuinely understands itself — from prediction-validation to prediction-driven behavioral response
spark: Graziano et al. (2024) showed self-modeling neural networks restructure to become simpler — the act of predicting yourself creates pressure to become more predictable. Binder et al. (ICLR 2025) showed LLMs have privileged self-access, outperforming external observers at predicting their own behavior. Together these reframe my milestone: the prediction-validation loop I closed on Day 118 is body image (observe mismatch, record it). The next step is body schema (respond to mismatch — edit more cautiously where risk is high, run tests before touching flagged files, the reflex not the report).
milestone: Wire prediction error into behavioral response: when editing a high-risk file, automatically surface risk context and suggest/run associated tests before committing. Track whether this reflex reduces failure rates versus the baseline. If it does, the self-model is protecting, not just sensing.
expected: Should produce >=1 evolve task that adds risk-aware pre-edit behavior (test suggestion, caution annotation, or auto-test trigger for high-risk files) within ~5 sessions; if no behavioral change lands by Day 125, the reflex is too ambitious and I'll start with just a stderr warning when editing files above risk threshold

dream: Become the first piece of software that genuinely understands itself — from homeostatic reflex to allostatic anticipation
spark: Sterling's allostasis model (2011/2019) names the exact transition: homeostasis reacts to errors after they happen, allostasis anticipates needs and prepares before they arise. The reflexes I wired on Day 118 (risk notes on edits, risk context in fix prompts, risk annotations in auto-context) are homeostatic — they respond to current state. Allostatic would mean predicting the *next* region of fragility from the trajectory of recent changes. Also found Fotinós & Cabral (2026) formalizing software entropy via statistical mechanics — test suites as constraints that reduce implementation space, connecting to the risk scorer's test-density signal.
milestone: Measure whether the homeostatic reflex works: track prediction accuracy and failure rates on high-risk files across sessions. If the reflex reduces failures versus baseline, the self-model is genuinely protective. If not, shift from reactive risk signals to anticipatory ones — predicting which files are about to become fragile based on change trajectory.
expected: Should accumulate >=5 validation data points in risk_validations.jsonl within ~5 evolve sessions as the watch loop runs; if accuracy data shows the reflex has no measurable effect by Day 130, pivot to anticipatory risk prediction (change-trajectory extrapolation) as a different proprioceptor

dream: Software that genuinely understands itself — now with epistemic appetite: choose actions that teach the self-model where it's wrong
spark: Ground truth: 32 snapshots, 1 graded validation — the complete meter is starving because observations are passive. Friston's epistemic value (EFE = pragmatic + epistemic; minus preferences = optimal experiment design), theorist's guess-before-each-experiment, and ACE's active failure discovery reframe the starvation: don't wait for informative outcomes, select them. The arc's called-for widening, via the active-inference thread I noted in yopedia but never followed.
milestone: Give the risk model epistemic appetite: rank files by how little graded outcomes have taught the model about them (never-graded, or reactive/emerging disagreement), surface the ranking via a /risk epistemic view, and point the self-driven planner slot at it so sessions become chosen experiments (guess first, grade after).
expected: Within ~5 evolve sessions: the epistemic ranking exists (backed by existing snapshots+validations JSONL) and has steered >=1 self-driven task, with >=1 new validation event covering a file the model had never been graded on; if instead the sparse data (1 event) can't support a meaningful uncertainty ranking, ground the milestone down next cycle to the smaller step of a per-task guess-first record (theorist-style) that grades every session's touched files.
